考研英语外刊精读

Silicon Valley Has Lost Its Biggest Advantage

硅谷已失去最大优势：AI 正从数字产业变身为重工业

来源：The Atlantic 原文日期：July 18, 2026 精读生成：2026-07-19 话题：科技 / 商业

文章大意

本文出自《大西洋月刊》2026 年 7 月 18 日科技版块，聚焦人工智能产业正在经历的深刻转型：曾经以软件、算法和人才为核心优势的硅谷，如今发现 AI 竞争的关键不再是“比特”，而是“原子和电子”——电力、芯片、铜、硅、冷却系统、土地与劳动力等实体资源。文章以 Caterpillar 因数据中心建设而股价大涨为切入点，揭示科技巨头为训练与运行 AI 模型，正投入超过运营收入的巨资建设数据中心，甚至需要举债。数据中心日益成为能耗惊人的重工业设施，科技高管的言辞也开始像建筑商。作者指出，从电力、供应链到金融工具，AI 已“变形成”一种重工业，硅谷过去“低成本、高回报”的数字优势正在消失。

阅读技巧提示

  • 本文是“现象—原因—影响”结构：先用 Caterpillar 股价异动作引子，再分析数据中心建设的实体资源需求，最后得出“AI 已成为重工业”的结论。
  • 注意数字与比较：文中大量出现 megawatt、gigawatt、trillion 等量化表达，阅读时把它们与“家庭用电量”“城市用电量”等类比联系起来，更容易理解规模。
  • 词义猜测题常考同义替换：如 “electrons” 在文中即 “power”；“tokens” 被解释为 “words”；“morphed into a heavy industry” 可替换为 “transformed into a capital-intensive industry”。
  • 长难句多含插入语、同位语和定语从句，先找主谓宾，再处理修饰成分。
  • 关注作者态度：对 AI 产业“重工业化”既有 awe（惊叹）也有 alarm（担忧），这种矛盾语气是理解全文基调的关键。

正文精读

The AI boom has showered some of the nation's most prominentadj. /ˈprɒmɪnənt/
杰出的；重要的 companies in market value. OpenAI and Anthropic are now the two most valuable private companies in the world. Google, Microsoft, and Nvidia have become larger than ever. But among the biggest winners has been Caterpillar, a purveyorn. /pəˈveɪə(r)/
供应商；提供者 of yellow trucks and cranes. Caterpillar's stock has more than doubled in value over the past year, making the company worth six times as much as Nike. Its ascentn. /əˈsent/
上升；攀升 has little to do with the construction vehicles (both real and toy) that it is known for. Instead, Caterpillar's success comes from its giant gas-powered engines that are helping power the nation's data-center build-outn. /ˈbɪld aʊt/
（基础设施）扩建；部署.

For all the attention given to improving model capabilities, the most important inputsn. /ˈɪnpʊts/
投入（物）；输入 to AI are not bits but atoms and electrons. To train and run its models, the AI industry needs to build. Data centers are incredibly complex technological and industrial operations—they require tech companies to erectv. /ɪˈrekt/
建造；搭建 power plants, build wastewater facilitiesn. /fəˈsɪlətiz/
设施；设备, and set up all manner of electrical equipment, power lines, and advanced-cooling equipment. That calls for concrete, steel, silicon, glass, copper, and liquefied natural gas.

This data-center construction has long been frenziedadj. /ˈfrenzid/
狂热的；疯狂的, but now it is fast approaching an inflection pointn. /ɪnˈflekʃn pɔɪnt/
拐点；转折点 that is both awesome and alarming. The major players—Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle—are on trackphr. /ɒn træk/
按计划；有望 to spend more on data centers by the end of the year than they bring in from their operations. That means tech giants whose non-chatbot businesses bring in tens of billions of dollars every year will likely need to take on debtphr. /teɪk ɒn det/
承担债务；举债 to keep paying for their AI ambitions. From the launch of ChatGPT, in late 2022, through the end of last year, these firms' capital expendituresn. /ɪkˈspendɪtʃəz/
支出；花费—most of which go toward data centers—well exceededv. /ɪkˈsiːdɪd/
超过；超出 half a trillion dollars. The companies intend to spend a similar amount in 2026 alone. And next year, their AI investments will likely exceed $1.1 trillion, according to a recent report from J.P. Morgan.

Silicon Valley has long enjoyed a straightforward advantage: Its products and services cost relatively little to make but generate tremendousadj. /trəˈmendəs/
巨大的；惊人的 revenuesn. /ˈrevənjuːz/
收入；收益. But operating a data center is in many ways more like running a steel mill than a smartphone app. In particularphr. /ɪn pəˈtɪkjələ(r)/
尤其；特别, data centers require stunningadj. /ˈstʌnɪŋ/
惊人的；极大的 amounts of electricity, especially as AI models have gotten more powerful. Five years ago, a standard data center might require 10 or 50 megawatts of power, enough for tens of thousands of homes. A year ago, data centers demanding a gigawatt or two of power were considered gargantuanadj. /ɡɑːˈɡæntʃuən/
庞大的；巨大的. This week, Meta announced that it is more than doubling the size of its flagshipn. /ˈflæɡʃɪp/
旗舰（项目/产品） AI data center, which will increase its peak power needs to five gigawatts. A data center proposed in Utah, if completed, will demand nine gigawatts of power. That's several large cities' worth of energy pulsing through a few big warehouses, all dedicated tophr. /ˈdedɪkeɪtɪd tuː/
专门用于；致力于 bots.

Electrical grids typically don't have enough energy to supply these megaprojectsn. /ˈmeɡəprɒdʒekts/
大型工程；巨型项目, and so the fastest way to bring a data center online is to build your own power plant. That's where Caterpillar comes in: The company has an enormous number of back ordersn. /ˈbæk ˈɔːdəz/
积压订单；待交付订单 for its power equipment, as does every major natural-gas-turbine manufacturer in the world. In May, Elon Musk reportedly spent at least $1 billion to buy an energy company with a fleet of combustion turbines, likely to power his AI model, Grok.

Power is only one part of the challenge. A data center requires wiring together dozens of cutting-edgeadj. /ˌkʌtɪŋ ˈedʒ/
尖端的；最先进的 AI chips onto a refrigerator-size rack, then linking thousands of those racks in a building. Those chips run hot, up to 200 degrees Fahrenheit, and are typically cooled by some combination of water and industrial fans; get too close to one of the racks, and you may break a sweat.

OpenAI's self-proclaimed goal of making "intelligence cheaper, better, and more abundantadj. /əˈbʌndənt/
丰富的；充足的 over time" really amounts tophr. /əˈmaʊnts tuː/
相当于；意味着 powering and cooling data centers as efficiently as possible—in other words, getting really good at HVAC. And then there's the complex global supply chain of mirrors and lasers and rare-earth minerals that go into the chips used to train and run AI models.

As the AI industry pours more money than ever before into data centers, executives have begun sounding like they are in the construction business. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has said that the biggest constraintn. /kənˈstreɪnt/
限制；约束 on his company is "electrons"—that is, power. Musk, Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, and others have said the same. Meanwhile, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang recently said that a lack of skilled "plumbers and electricians" will be the AI boom's "hardest" bottleneckn. /ˈbɒtlnek/
瓶颈；障碍 to overcome.

Still, Silicon Valley seems to have only just begun reckoning withphr. /ˈrekənɪŋ wɪð/
认真应对；正视 what it means for its primary business operations to be in the material realm. You cannot vibe-code a data center. In perhaps the clearest sign of the AI industry's desperation to keep building, Anthropic, the most safety-conscious firm, is spending $1 billion a month to rent a data center from Musk, who just a few months ago deemedv. /diːmd/
认为；视为（deem 的过去式） Anthropic "evil." Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, for his part, has accused Musk of "disturbing negligencen. /ˈneɡlɪdʒəns/
疏忽；过失." Meanwhile, the tech industry is struggling to quellv. /kwel/
平息；压制 a mountingadj. /ˈmaʊntɪŋ/
不断增加的；上升的 AI backlashn. /ˈbæklæʃ/
强烈反对；抵制, which has largely taken the form of opposition to data centers. A few days ago, New York became the first state to institute a moratoriumn. /ˌmɒrəˈtɔːriəm/
暂停；中止 on new "hyperscale" data-center construction.

If anything, AI is poisedadj. /pɔɪzd/
准备好的；蓄势待发的 to become even more like drilling an oil well. All the copper, silicon, electricity, and labor that go into building and running a data center are musteredv. /ˈmʌstəd/
召集；调动 to process and spit out "tokensn. /ˈtəʊkənz/
（AI 产出的）基本单位；代币；令牌（复数）"—in essence, words—that are the basic unit of AI. The State Department recently announced an international pact to bolsterv. /ˈbəʊlstə(r)/
加强；支持 the AI supply chain, called Pax Silica. "If the 20th century ran on oil and steel, the 21st century runs on compute and the minerals that feed it," Jacob Helberg, the undersecretary for economic affairs, said in the announcement. Financial markets and investors are already beginning to treat tokens as the rawadj. /rɔː/
原始的；未加工的 inputs to modern civilization: Various firms are launching such markets for compute, akin tophr. /əˈkɪn tuː/
类似于；与…相似 how you can trade oil, steel, and corn on a stock exchange.

By dominatingv. /ˈdɒmɪneɪtɪŋ/
主导；控制 the digital world, Silicon Valley has already exertedv. /ɪɡˈzɜːtɪd/
施加（影响、压力等） indirect control over the physical one. Today's data-center build-out is a whole new beast. From the most concrete aspects—cooling, power, land—to the most abstract financial instruments, AI has morphed intophr. /mɔːft ˈɪntuː/
转变为；变成 a heavy industry.

点击红色划线词汇或短语可查看中文释义。

短语搭配

on track
按计划；有望
"The major players—Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle—are on track to spend more on data centers by the end of the year than they bring in from their operations."
take on debt
承担债务；举债
"That means tech giants whose non-chatbot businesses bring in tens of billions of dollars every year will likely need to take on debt to keep paying for their AI ambitions."
in particular
尤其；特别
"In particular, data centers require stunning amounts of electricity, especially as AI models have gotten more powerful."
dedicated to
专门用于；致力于
"That's several large cities' worth of energy pulsing through a few big warehouses, all dedicated to bots."
amounts to
相当于；意味着
"OpenAI's self-proclaimed goal really amounts to powering and cooling data centers as efficiently as possible."
pour ... into
把（大量资金等）投入…
"As the AI industry pours more money than ever before into data centers, executives have begun sounding like they are in the construction business."
reckoning with
认真应对；正视
"Silicon Valley seems to have only just begun reckoning with what it means for its primary business operations to be in the material realm."
akin to
类似于；与…相似
"Financial markets are launching such markets for compute, akin to how you can trade oil, steel, and corn on a stock exchange."

长难句解析

The major players—Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle—are on track to spend more on data centers by the end of the year than they bring in from their operations.

结构分析：主语为 The major players，破折号之间为同位语，具体列举五大科技巨头；谓语为 are on track to spend，其中 on track to do sth. 表示“有望/按计划做某事”；宾语为 more on data centers，其后 than 引导比较状语从句，比较对象是它们从日常运营中获得的收入。

参考译文：亚马逊、谷歌、微软、Meta 和甲骨文等主要玩家今年年底之前在数据中心上的支出有望超过其日常运营所带来的收入。

That means tech giants whose non-chatbot businesses bring in tens of billions of dollars every year will likely need to take on debt to keep paying for their AI ambitions.

结构分析：主句为 That means + 宾语从句；宾语从句的主语是 tech giants，其后 whose 引导定语从句修饰 tech giants；谓语为 will likely need to take on debt；不定式 to keep paying for their AI ambitions 作目的状语。

参考译文：这意味着那些非聊天机器人业务每年带来数百亿美元收入的科技巨头，很可能需要举债以继续为其 AI 雄心买单。

This week, Meta announced that it is more than doubling the size of its flagship AI data center, which will increase its peak power needs to five gigawatts.

结构分析：主句为 Meta announced that...；that 引导宾语从句，从句中 it is more than doubling... 为现在进行时表计划中的动作；which 引导非限制性定语从句，修饰前面整个宾语从句内容；increase...to... 表示“把…增加到…”。

参考译文：本周，Meta 宣布其旗舰 AI 数据中心的规模将增加一倍以上，这将使其峰值电力需求达到 50 亿瓦。

All the copper, silicon, electricity, and labor that go into building and running a data center are mustered to process and spit out “tokens”—in essence, words—that are the basic unit of AI.

结构分析：主语为 All the copper, silicon, electricity, and labor，其后 that go into building and running a data center 为定语从句；谓语为 are mustered to process and spit out；宾语为 tokens；in essence, words 为同位语，解释 tokens；其后 that are the basic unit of AI 为定语从句修饰 tokens。

参考译文：建造和运行数据中心所需的所有铜、硅、电力和劳动力，都被调动起来处理和产出“token”——本质上就是单词——即 AI 的基本单位。

阅读理解练习

1. What is the main argument of the article?

  • A. Caterpillar has overtaken Nike to become the world's most valuable company.
  • B. The AI industry is shifting from a software-driven model to a capital-intensive, heavy-industry model.
  • C. Tech giants should stop building data centers to avoid a financial crisis.
  • D. New York's moratorium on data centers will solve the AI backlash.

正确答案：B

解析：文章开篇以 Caterpillar 因数据中心建设而股价大涨为例，指出 AI 竞争的关键不再是软件和人才，而是电力、芯片、原材料等实体基础设施；随后论证数据中心建设正使科技巨头变成重资产、重工业型企业。A 项与原文“Caterpillar 市值是 Nike 的六倍”混淆；C、D 项并非作者主张。

2. Why does the author mention Caterpillar at the beginning of the article?

  • A. To praise Caterpillar's new line of toy construction vehicles.
  • B. To show that the AI boom benefits unexpected companies in the physical economy.
  • C. To argue that Caterpillar should invest more in AI software.
  • D. To compare Caterpillar with Nike in terms of market value.

正确答案：B

解析：作者指出 Caterpillar 股价上涨并非因为卡车或玩具，而是其燃气发动机为数据中心扩建供电，说明 AI 繁荣正在惠及实体经济中意想不到的重工业公司，从而引出“AI 已变成重工业”的主题。

3. What can be inferred from the statement “AI has morphed into a heavy industry”?

  • A. AI companies no longer need software engineers.
  • B. AI development now depends heavily on physical resources and industrial infrastructure.
  • C. Heavy industries are being replaced by AI technology.
  • D. AI will soon become less important than oil and steel.

正确答案：B

解析：全文反复强调数据中心需要电力、土地、铜、硅、冷却系统、管道工和电工等实体资源，可见 AI 已从代码主导转向依赖物理基础设施的重工业模式。A、C、D 项均与文意不符或过度推断。

考研核心词汇表

单词/短语 词性 中文释义 例句
prominent
/ˈprɒmɪnənt/		 adj. 杰出的；重要的 The AI boom has showered some of the nation's most prominent companies in market value.
purveyor
/pəˈveɪə(r)/		 n. 供应商；提供者 But among the biggest winners has been Caterpillar, a purveyor of yellow trucks and cranes.
ascent
/əˈsent/		 n. 上升；攀升 Its ascent has little to do with the construction vehicles that it is known for.
build-out
/ˈbɪld aʊt/		 n. （基础设施）扩建；部署 Caterpillar's success comes from engines that are helping power the nation's data-center build-out.
input
/ˈɪnpʊt/		 n. 投入（物）；输入 The most important inputs to AI are not bits but atoms and electrons.
erect
/ɪˈrekt/		 v. 建造；搭建 Data centers require tech companies to erect power plants and build wastewater facilities.
facility
/fəˈsɪləti/		 n. 设施；设备 They require tech companies to build wastewater facilities and set up electrical equipment.
frenzied
/ˈfrenzid/		 adj. 狂热的；疯狂的 This data-center construction has long been frenzied.
inflection point
/ɪnˈflekʃn pɔɪnt/		 n. 拐点；转折点 It is fast approaching an inflection point that is both awesome and alarming.
on track
/ɒn træk/		 phr. 按计划；有望 The major players are on track to spend more on data centers than they bring in from operations.
expenditure
/ɪkˈspendɪtʃə(r)/		 n. 支出；花费 These firms' capital expenditures well exceeded half a trillion dollars.
exceed
/ɪkˈsiːd/		 v. 超过；超出 Capital expenditures well exceeded half a trillion dollars.
tremendous
/trəˈmendəs/		 adj. 巨大的；惊人的 Its products cost relatively little to make but generate tremendous revenues.
revenue
/ˈrevənjuː/		 n. 收入；收益 Its products and services generate tremendous revenues.
in particular
/ɪn pəˈtɪkjələ(r)/		 phr. 尤其；特别 In particular, data centers require stunning amounts of electricity.
stunning
/ˈstʌnɪŋ/		 adj. 惊人的；极大的 Data centers require stunning amounts of electricity.
gargantuan
/ɡɑːˈɡæntʃuən/		 adj. 庞大的；巨大的 A year ago, data centers demanding a gigawatt or two were considered gargantuan.
flagship
/ˈflæɡʃɪp/		 n. 旗舰（项目/产品） Meta announced that it is more than doubling the size of its flagship AI data center.
dedicate to
/ˈdedɪkeɪt tuː/		 phr. 专门用于；致力于 That's several large cities' worth of energy, all dedicated to bots.
megaproject
/ˈmeɡəprɒdʒekt/		 n. 大型工程；巨型项目 Electrical grids typically don't have enough energy to supply these megaprojects.
back order
/ˈbæk ˈɔːdə(r)/		 n. 积压订单；待交付订单 The company has an enormous number of back orders for its power equipment.
cutting-edge
/ˌkʌtɪŋ ˈedʒ/		 adj. 尖端的；最先进的 A data center requires wiring together dozens of cutting-edge AI chips.
abundant
/əˈbʌndənt/		 adj. 丰富的；充足的 OpenAI's goal is to make intelligence cheaper, better, and more abundant over time.
amount to
/əˈmaʊnt tuː/		 phr. 相当于；意味着 That really amounts to powering and cooling data centers as efficiently as possible.
constraint
/kənˈstreɪnt/		 n. 限制；约束 Sam Altman has said that the biggest constraint on his company is 'electrons'.
bottleneck
/ˈbɒtlnek/		 n. 瓶颈；障碍 A lack of skilled plumbers and electricians will be the AI boom's hardest bottleneck to overcome.
reckon with
/ˈrekən wɪð/		 phr. 认真应对；正视 Silicon Valley seems to have only just begun reckoning with what it means to be in the material realm.
deem
/diːm/		 v. 认为；视为 Musk just a few months ago deemed Anthropic 'evil'.
negligence
/ˈneɡlɪdʒəns/		 n. 疏忽；过失 Dario Amodei has accused Musk of 'disturbing negligence'.
quell
/kwel/		 v. 平息；压制 The tech industry is struggling to quell a mounting AI backlash.
mounting
/ˈmaʊntɪŋ/		 adj. 不断增加的；上升的 The tech industry is struggling to quell a mounting AI backlash.
backlash
/ˈbæklæʃ/		 n. 强烈反对；抵制 The tech industry is struggling to quell a mounting AI backlash.
moratorium
/ˌmɒrəˈtɔːriəm/		 n. 暂停；中止 New York became the first state to institute a moratorium on new hyperscale data-center construction.
poised
/pɔɪzd/		 adj. 准备好的；蓄势待发的 AI is poised to become even more like drilling an oil well.
muster
/ˈmʌstə(r)/		 v. 召集；调动 All the copper, silicon, electricity, and labor are mustered to process and spit out tokens.
token
/ˈtəʊkən/		 n. （AI 产出的）基本单位；代币 All those inputs are mustered to process and spit out 'tokens'—in essence, words.
in essence
/ɪn ˈesns/		 phr. 本质上；实质上 'Tokens'—in essence, words—are the basic unit of AI.
bolster
/ˈbəʊlstə(r)/		 v. 加强；支持 The State Department announced an international pact to bolster the AI supply chain.
raw
/rɔː/		 adj. 原始的；未加工的 Investors are beginning to treat tokens as the raw inputs to modern civilization.
akin to
/əˈkɪn tuː/		 phr. 类似于；与…相似 Markets for compute are akin to how you can trade oil, steel, and corn on a stock exchange.
dominate
/ˈdɒmɪneɪt/		 v. 主导；控制 By dominating the digital world, Silicon Valley has exerted indirect control over the physical one.
exert
/ɪɡˈzɜːt/		 v. 施加（影响、压力等） Silicon Valley has already exerted indirect control over the physical world.
morph into
/mɔːf ˈɪntuː/		 phr. 转变为；变成 AI has morphed into a heavy industry.

Generated by Trae Work | 考研英语外刊精读

原文来源：The Atlantic, July 18, 2026
